evidence: The statement's hypothesis is that every arithmetic progression contains infinitely many integers which are the sum of distinct a_i.
The conclusion 'every sufficiently large integer is the sum of distinct a_i' is the completeness property of the sequence.
The problem asks when 'every sufficiently large integer is the sum of distinct elements of A', i.e. when A is (eventually) complete.
Cassels [Ca60] proved that under stronger hypotheses every sufficiently large integer is the sum of distinct elements of A.
The problem asks whether the sum over any infinite A of 1/(2^n - 1) is irrational.
The sum of d(n)/2^n equals the sum of 1/(2^n-1), the Erdős–Borwein constant, whose irrationality Erdős proved [Er48].
The statement is the irrationality of the sum of d(n)/2^n 'where d(n) is the number of divisors of n'.
The series in question is the sum of d(n)/(a_1...a_n) 'where d(n) is the number of divisors of n'.
The problem asks whether the sum of d(n)/(a_1...a_n) is irrational; proved affirmatively by Chojecki and GPT-5.4 Pro [Ch26].
The series is the sum of mu(n)^2 * n / 2^n with mu the Möbius function (open scoped ArithmeticFunction.Moebius).
The problem asks whether the sum of mu(n)^2 n/2^n is irrational, proved by Chen and Ruzsa [ChRu99].
The weight mu(n)^2 in the series is the indicator of the square-free integers.
The Behrend property asked for is that the set of multiples of {a+k : a in A} has natural density 1 ('almost all integers have a divisor of the form a+k').
The problem asks whether the sum of a_n/2^{a_n} is irrational whenever a_n/n tends to infinity.
An irrationality sequence is defined by the sum of 1/b_n converging to an irrational number; part (i) asks whether 2^{2^n} is one.
Part (ii) asks whether every irrationality sequence (sums of 1/b_n irrational) must satisfy a_n^{1/n} tending to infinity.
An irrationality sequence here requires the sum of 1/(a_n+b_n) to be irrational for every bounded perturbation b_n; part (i) is that 2^n is not one (Kovac-Tao).
Part (ii) asks whether n! is an irrationality sequence, i.e. whether the perturbed reciprocal sums are always irrational.
The docstring asks 'Is n! an example of an irrationality sequence?', stated via Nat.factorial.
